channel.title: Evan Rowbotham on
channel.description: Recent content in Evan Rowbotham on

item.title: Applied Physics Research
item.description:
These past five months, I have been working in The Billinge Group. Broadly, I have been contributing to solving the nanostructure problem, through maintaining and deploying packages (old and new) within our Atomic Structure Analysis Software, Diffpy.

  • Lead the deployment and maintenance pdffit2 and pdfgui.
  • Documenting and formalizing a streamlined software deployment process for all current & future packages within the group with Andrew Yang.
  • Collaborating with Ibrhaim Adnan on designing novel CV algorithms for classifying x-ray diffraction data here. (our repo will soon be public)

item.title: RL Trading Model
item.description:
Under Construction.

Over the summer I got work as a data science intern for a crypto hedge fund, Floating Point Group.

  • ADD

item.title: Optimal way to play Blackjack
item.description:
This was an independent study conducted under Farez Siddiqui, through the Math Department’s Directed Reading Program (DRP).

  • Researched reinforment learning with an emphasis on applications in finance. My primary research text was Barto and Sutton’s Reinforcement Learning.
  • Designed an RL model using monte carlo simulations to find the optimal policy for playing blackjack. You can find it on my github, here.
  • Presented my project at FSU’s Undergraduate Research Symposium.

item.title: The Age-Wisdom Paradox
item.description:
I heard a friend’s parent recently said something that piqued my interest, “If I do not know my job by now, [20 years] shame on me.” Well, what would I, a nineteen-year-old, know about life’s struggles, professional obstacles, and ? Besides a short internship, probably nothing tangible or directly applicable.

Although, there is something most older people overlook. My claim is that age is not an indicator of wisdom, but rather wisdom is proportional to time spent in reflection. Age is a forcing function that makes us to come to grips with own morality. Our wrestle with finiteness, is what makes one wise, I believe.

item.title: Why Math?
item.description: Initially, I wanted to study philosophy at university. Philosophy, I thought was the closest I could get to the two things I have been chasing my entire life: truth and understanding. But then my Dad was nervous about me finding a job and heavily encouraged me to study something else. So, I decided to study what people always told me I had a knack for and for what I thought was as close as I could get to philosophy, math. To be a philosopher is to attempt to formalize visceral and intuitive experiences into precise language and axioms; replace experiences with phenomena, and language with abstract symbols, and we pretty much arrive at math.

item.title: Quotes
item.description:
“Do the best until you know better. Then when you know better, do better.” Maya Angelou

“Stay hungry. Stay foolish.” Steve Jobs

“In mathematics, our freedom lies in the questions we ask – and in how we pursue them – but not in the answers awaiting us.” Steven Strogatz

“Do. Or do not. There is no try.” Yoda
